notes: Old-recipe chef review wave. Sources stay internal. Public recipe pages should read as confident cooking guidance.

recipes.review_summary: Benachin is well supported as a Gambian one-pot rice dish related to thieboudienne. The old page had the right frame, but the ingredient list did not show oil, tomato, garlic, chile, salt, pepper, or cooking liquid clearly enough.
recommended_changes: Keep fish, rice, tomato paste, cassava, and sweet potato.
Add fresh tomato, garlic, oil, chile, salt, pepper, and cooking liquid.
Make the one-pot vegetable-and-rice timing clearer.
recipes.official_source_status: No official standard found. Gambian food references agree on benachin as one-pot rice with fish or meat, tomato, vegetables, and rice.

sources.title: Overseas Adventure Travel - Gambia food guide

recipe.description: Gambian one-pot tomato rice cooked with fish, cassava, sweet potato, cabbage, carrot, chile, and a rich seasoned broth.
recipe.story: Benachin means one pot, and the pot should taste complete before the rice goes in. Fry the fish, build a tomato broth, cook the vegetables, then let the rice absorb everything.
recipe.best_served_with: Lime wedges, fresh chile sauce, cucumber salad, or tapalapa bread
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks use fish, others use chicken or meat. Vegetables change with the market, but cassava, cabbage, carrot, and sweet potato are common companions.

replace_ingredients.name: sweet potato
replace_ingredients.prep_note: peeled and cut into large chunks

replace_ingredients.name: cassava
replace_ingredients.prep_note: peeled and cut into large chunks

replace_steps.title: Season and fry fish
replace_steps.instruction: Season fish with salt and pepper. Fry in hot oil until browned on both sides, then remove.

replace_steps.title: Build tomato broth
replace_steps.instruction: In the same pot, cook onions until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste, then tomatoes, hot pepper, and water or stock.

replace_steps.title: Cook vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add cassava, sweet potato, carrot, and cabbage. Simmer until just tender, then lift the vegetables out and keep warm.

replace_steps.title: Cook rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir rice into the broth. Place fish and vegetables on top, cover tightly, and cook on low until the rice absorbs the broth.

replace_steps.title: Serve family style
replace_steps.instruction: Rest 10 minutes, then serve the rice on a platter with fish and vegetables arranged over it. Add lime if using.

recipes.review_summary: Domoda is strongly supported as Gambian groundnut stew with beef or chicken, peanut paste, tomatoes, tomato paste, onion, pumpkin or sweet potato, chile, and rice. The old page needed salt, pepper, lemon, and water made visible.
recommended_changes: Keep beef, peanut butter, tomato paste, pumpkin, onion, and chile.
Add water or stock, fresh tomato, lemon or lime, salt, and pepper.
Let the stew simmer long enough for the peanut oil to rise.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Gambian references consistently describe domoda as groundnut stew served with rice.

sources.title: Daring Gourmet - Domoda Gambian Peanut Stew

recipe.description: Gambian groundnut stew with beef, tomato, pumpkin, chile, lemon, and a creamy peanut sauce served over rice.
recipe.story: Domoda should be rich but not rushed. Let the peanut sauce simmer until glossy and the pumpkin softens into the stew.
recipe.best_served_with: Plain white rice, lime wedges, cucumber, or steamed greens
recipe.regional_variations: Chicken, fish, beef, or vegetarian versions are all common. Pumpkin, sweet potato, okra, or bitter tomato can appear depending on the cook.

replace_steps.title: Brown the meat
replace_steps.instruction: Season beef with salt and pepper, then brown in a little oil. Remove and reserve.

replace_steps.title: Start the stew
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions and tomatoes until soft. Stir in tomato paste, water or stock, and the browned meat.

replace_steps.title: Add groundnut
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk peanut butter with warm broth from the pot, then stir it back in until smooth. Add pumpkin and whole chile.
replace_steps.tip: Loosening the peanut butter first keeps the sauce from clumping.

replace_steps.title: Simmer slowly
replace_steps.instruction: Cook on low heat until the meat and pumpkin are tender and the sauce is thick and glossy.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt, pepper, and lemon or lime. Serve hot over white rice.

recipes.review_summary: Supakanja is plausible and locally recognized as Gambian okra soup. The old page had the core okra, palm oil, dried fish, shrimp, and pepper pattern but needed salt, water, and optional leafy greens made explicit.
recommended_changes: Keep okra, palm oil, dried fish, seafood, and chile.
Add water or stock, salt, and optional greens.
Keep the draw texture as an expected feature, not a flaw.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Gambian cuisine references mention supakanja, and West African okra soup references support palm oil, okra, fish or seafood, and greens.

recipe.description: Gambian okra soup with palm oil, dried fish, shrimp, chile, and a silky draw texture for rice or fufu.
recipe.story: Supakanja should be silky and generous. Slice the okra finely, let it draw in the palm oil, then add fish and seafood without overcooking them.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, cassava, or rice balls
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks add leafy greens, crab, smoked fish, beef, or extra dried seafood.

replace_steps.title: Draw the okra
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and add sliced okra. Cook, stirring, until glossy and beginning to draw.

replace_steps.title: Add fish and aromatics
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, dried fish, and peppers. Stir gently until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Simmer
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or fish stock and simmer until the soup thickens. Add greens if using.

replace_steps.title: Finish seafood
replace_steps.instruction: Add shrimp or seafood and cook just until done. Season with salt.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve hot with rice or fufu, making sure each bowl has fish, okra, and broth.

recipes.review_summary: The Ghanaian jollof page is sound. The main gaps were missing salt, stock or water, bay leaf, and optional curry or thyme while the method depends on seasoning and liquid control.
recommended_changes: Keep basmati rice and tomato stew as the Ghanaian frame.
Add stock or water, salt, bay leaf, and optional curry or thyme.
Keep the tight-lid steaming method.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ghanaian and broader jollof references agree on basmati or long-grain rice, tomato stew, onion, oil, ginger, garlic, chile, and seasoning.

recipe.description: Ghanaian jollof rice with basmati rice steamed in a reduced tomato, onion, ginger, garlic, chile, and bay-leaf stew.
recipe.story: Ghanaian jollof is all about the stew before the rice. Fry the tomato base until it tastes rounded, then let basmati steam gently under a tight lid.
recipe.best_served_with: Shito, fried plantain, grilled chicken, salad, or kelewele
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks use curry powder, thyme, nutmeg, or stock cubes. The rice is often perfumed basmati or another long-grain rice.

replace_ingredients.name: canned tomatoes or fresh tomato puree

replace_steps.title: Blend the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes with one onion, garlic, and ginger until smooth.

replace_steps.title: Fry the tomato stew
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil, cook sliced onion, then fry tomato paste. Add blended sauce, bay leaves, chile, and optional curry or thyme. Cook until thick and glossy.

replace_steps.title: Add rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in washed rice until coated. Add hot stock or water and season well.

replace_steps.title: Steam
replace_steps.instruction: Cover tightly with foil and a lid. Cook on low until the rice is tender and the liquid is absorbed.

replace_steps.title: Rest and fluff
replace_steps.instruction: Rest off heat for 10 minutes, remove bay leaves and chile, then fluff gently.

recipes.review_summary: Ghanaian groundnut soup is sound as a peanut, tomato, chile, ginger, and chicken soup for fufu or rice balls. The old page needed stock/water, garlic, tomato paste, and salt/pepper visible.
recommended_changes: Keep peanut butter or groundnut paste, chicken, tomato, onion, ginger, and chile.
Add stock, garlic, tomato paste, salt, and pepper.
Keep the soup looser than stew so it works with fufu.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ghanaian recipes agree on groundnut paste, tomato, onion, ginger, chile, meat or fish, and fufu/rice-ball pairings.

sources.title: The Guardian - Ghanaian groundnut soup recipe
Ginger and Sea Salt - Groundnut Soup with Chicken

recipe.description: Ghanaian groundnut soup with chicken, peanut paste, tomato, ginger, chile, and a silky broth for fufu or rice balls.
recipe.story: Groundnut soup should pour like soup, not sit like stew. Cook the tomato base down, loosen the peanut paste with stock, then simmer until the oil shines gently on top.
recipe.best_served_with: Fufu, omo tuo, rice balls, boiled yam, or kokonte
recipe.regional_variations: Chicken, goat, fish, smoked fish, garden eggs, or okra may move through the pot depending on the household.

replace_ingredients.name: natural peanut butter or groundnut paste

replace_steps.title: Season chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt, pepper, onion, garlic, and ginger. Simmer with stock or water until partly tender.

replace_steps.title: Cook tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes and chile. Cook with tomato paste until reduced and no longer raw.

replace_steps.title: Loosen groundnut
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk peanut paste with hot stock until smooth, then add it to the soup.

replace_steps.title: Simmer soup
replace_steps.instruction: Return chicken and remaining stock to the pot. Simmer gently until the chicken is tender and the soup is silky.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt and serve hot with fufu, omo tuo, or yam.

recipes.review_summary: Kelewele is sound as Ghanaian spiced fried ripe plantain. The old page needed salt and the common garlic/onion spice paste option made visible.
recommended_changes: Keep ripe plantain, ginger, chile, cloves, nutmeg, and frying oil.
Add salt, garlic, and optional onion.
Keep the short marinade before frying.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ghanaian food references agree on ripe plantain, ginger, chile, cloves or warm spices, salt, and frying.

sources.title: University of Ghana handbook - Kelewele recipe mention

recipe.description: Ghanaian ripe plantain cubes marinated with ginger, garlic, chile, cloves, nutmeg, and salt, then fried until caramelized.
recipe.story: Kelewele should hit sweet, hot, and warm-spiced at once. Let the plantain sit briefly in the paste so the edges fry dark and fragrant.
recipe.best_served_with: Roasted peanuts, grilled meat, beans, red red, or a cold malt drink
recipe.regional_variations: Some spice pastes include onion, anise, grains of selim, or calabash nutmeg.

replace_steps.title: Make spice paste
replace_steps.instruction: Mix ginger, garlic, onion if using, chile, cloves, nutmeg, and salt into a rough paste.

replace_steps.title: Season plantain
replace_steps.instruction: Toss plantain cubes with the spice paste and rest briefly.

replace_steps.title: Fry in batches
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and fry plantain in batches until dark golden and crisp at the edges.

replace_steps.title: Drain and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Drain well and serve hot with roasted peanuts or beans.

recipes.review_summary: The fouti page is directionally sound as a Guinean fonio staple. The main issue was the default butter conflicting with vegan and dairy-free tags.
recommended_changes: Keep fonio, water, salt, and sauce serving guidance.
Use oil as the default fat so the recipe stays plant-based.
Keep the grain light rather than overworked.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Guinean cuisine references mention Foutti Fogne from Fouta, and fonio is strongly associated with Guinea and West Africa.

recipe.description: Light steamed fonio shaped into a soft mound for Guinean sauces, especially leaf sauce or peanut sauce.
recipe.story: Fouti is a gentle staple. Rinse the fonio well, steam it lightly, and shape it only enough to carry the sauce.
recipe.best_served_with: Sauce feuille, peanut sauce, fish stew, or tomato vegetable sauce
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks enrich the fonio with extra fat, but oil keeps the dish plant-based.

replace_steps.title: Wash the fonio
replace_steps.instruction: Rinse fonio in several changes of water until the water runs clear. Drain thoroughly.
replace_steps.tip: Use a fine-mesh sieve because fonio is tiny.

replace_steps.title: Cook the fonio
replace_steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil with salt and vegetable oil. Add the fonio, stir once, cover, and reduce heat to low.

replace_steps.title: Steam and fluff
replace_steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let steam covered. Fluff gently so the grains stay separate and light.

replace_steps.title: Shape and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Pack fonio into a wet bowl and turn out as a neat mound. Serve alongside sauces and stews.

recipes.review_summary: Yassa is originally associated with Casamance/Senegal but is widely cooked across nearby West Africa, including Guinea. The old page had the lemon-onion-chicken structure but missed garlic, mustard, salt, pepper, and bay in ingredients.
recommended_changes: Keep chicken, onion, citrus, ginger, chile, and oil.
Add garlic, mustard, bay leaf, salt, and pepper.
Keep browning the chicken before simmering in the onion sauce.
recipes.official_source_status: No official Guinean standard found. West African yassa references agree on chicken, onions, lemon or lime, mustard, garlic, chile, and rice.

recipe.description: Guinean-style chicken yassa with lime, ginger, mustard, garlic, hot pepper, and plenty of slow-cooked onions.
recipe.story: A good yassa is onion first. Marinate the chicken sharply, brown it well, then let the onions collapse into a sweet-tangy sauce.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed white rice, fried plantains, or a simple cucumber salad
recipe.regional_variations: Senegalese, Gambian, and Guinean versions overlap, with ginger and chile levels shifting by cook.

replace_steps.title: Marinate
replace_steps.instruction: Combine chicken with onions, citrus juice, mustard, garlic, ginger, peppers, bay leaf if using, salt, and pepper. Chill for at least 2 hours.

replace_steps.title: Brown chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Lift chicken from the marinade. Brown it in hot oil on all sides, then remove.

replace_steps.title: Cook onions
replace_steps.instruction: Add the onions and marinade to the pot. Cook until the onions are very soft and golden.

replace_steps.title: Simmer
replace_steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot, cover, and simmer until tender and the onion sauce is thick.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt and acidity. Serve over white rice.

recipes.review_summary: Sauce feuille is sound as a West African leaf sauce, and Guinean sources support leafy sauces. The old page needed garlic, water or stock, and salt/pepper visible.
recommended_changes: Keep sweet potato leaves, palm oil, smoked fish, and ground peanuts.
Add garlic, water or stock, salt, and pepper.
Keep the sauce thick enough for rice or fouti.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Regional leaf sauce references support cassava, sweet potato, or spinach leaves with palm oil, fish, and peanuts.

recipe.description: Guinean leaf sauce with sweet potato leaves, palm oil, smoked fish, ground peanuts, garlic, and hot pepper.
recipe.story: Sauce feuille should be green, smoky, and rounded. Cook the leaves until tender, then let peanuts and palm oil thicken the pot.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fouti, cassava, or boiled plantain
recipe.regional_variations: Cassava leaves, sweet potato leaves, spinach, or mixed greens can be used depending on what is available.

replace_ingredients.name: sweet potato leaves or cassava leaves

replace_steps.title: Cook leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer chopped leaves in salted water until tender, then drain lightly.

replace_steps.title: Start the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Cook onion and garlic until soft, then add smoked fish.

replace_steps.title: Add peanuts
replace_steps.instruction: Mix ground peanuts with water or stock, then stir into the pot with the leaves and peppers.

replace_steps.title: Simmer thick
replace_steps.instruction: Cook gently until the sauce thickens and the greens are fully tender.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt and pepper. Serve hot over rice or with fouti.

recipes.review_summary: Caldo de mancarra is strongly supported as Guinea-Bissau peanut broth or stew with chicken or fish, vegetables, and rice. The old page needed garlic, ginger, stock, oil, salt, and pepper visible.
recommended_changes: Keep chicken, peanuts, tomato, onion, sweet potato, and hot pepper.
Add garlic, ginger, oil, stock, salt, and pepper.
Allow the groundnut broth to stay pourable for rice.
recipes.official_source_status: Tourism and food references list caldo de mancarra as a characteristic Guinea-Bissau peanut broth with meat or fish.

recipe.description: Guinea-Bissau peanut broth with chicken, tomato, sweet potato, ginger, chile, and a creamy mancarra sauce for rice.
recipe.story: Caldo de mancarra should feel like a broth that became generous. Keep the peanut smooth, the chicken tender, and the rice ready to drink up the sauce.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, lime, cilantro, or boiled cassava
recipe.regional_variations: Chicken, fish, shrimp, okra, carrot, or sweet potato may appear depending on the cook and coast.

replace_steps.title: Season and brown chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and pepper. Brown in oil, then remove.

replace_steps.title: Cook the base
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes until soft and saucy.

replace_steps.title: Add peanut broth
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk peanut paste with warm stock, then add to the pot with remaining stock, chicken, sweet potatoes, and hot pepper.

replace_steps.title: Simmer
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer gently until the chicken is tender and the sweet potato is soft.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Taste for salt and pepper. Serve hot over white rice.

recipes.review_summary: Cashews are central to Guinea-Bissau agriculture and food culture, but arroz de caju has thinner recipe-source coverage than the major national dishes. The page is useful as a cashew rice side, but it should avoid dairy-free conflict from default butter.
recommended_changes: Keep rice and cashews.
Use oil as the default fat and butter only as optional.
Add salt and a lighter story that ties to cashew culture without overclaiming.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Guinea-Bissau references support cashews as a major crop and ingredient, but not a single fixed cashew rice formula.

sources.title: Insights into cashew production in Guinea-Bissau

recipe.description: Long-grain rice folded with toasted cashews, onion, and light aromatics as a Guinea-Bissau-inspired side.
recipe.story: Cashews are one of Guinea-Bissau's defining crops. Keep this rice simple so the toasted nuts stay the point.
recipe.best_served_with: Grilled chicken, fish stew, caldo de mancarra, or tomato salad
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks use plain oil, others enrich the rice with butter or stock.

replace_steps.title: Toast cashews
replace_steps.instruction: Toast cashews in a dry pan until golden and fragrant. Remove and reserve.

replace_steps.title: Start rice
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until soft. Add rice and stir until lightly coated.

replace_steps.title: Cook
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or stock and salt. Cover and cook until the rice is tender.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Rest 10 minutes, fluff, and fold in toasted cashews. Add butter only if using.

recipes.review_summary: The old page treated chabeu as jollof-style rice, but stronger Guinea-Bissau references describe caldo de chabeu as a palm-oil broth or stew with okra and meat or fish. The public recipe needed a full correction.
recommended_changes: Change from rice dish to palm-oil okra stew/broth.
Use fish or meat, palm oil, okra, tomato, onion, chile, and stock.
Serve with rice instead of cooking rice as the dish.
recipes.official_source_status: Tourism and encyclopedic references list caldo de chabeu as a characteristic Guinea-Bissau dish made with palm oil, okra, meat or fish.

recipe.name: Caldo de Chabeu
recipe.description: Guinea-Bissau palm-oil broth with okra, tomato, onion, chile, and fish or meat, served over rice.
recipe.story: Chabeu is not rice pretending to be jollof. It is the sauce, red from palm oil and thickened with okra, made to ladle generously over rice.
recipe.best_served_with: Plain white rice, cassava, or boiled plantain
recipe.regional_variations: Fish, dried fish, chicken, beef, or shellfish can anchor the broth depending on the coast and household.

replace_steps.title: Start the palm base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onions until soft. Add garlic, tomatoes, and hot pepper.

replace_steps.title: Add okra
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in okra and cook until it begins to thicken the oil and tomato base.

replace_steps.title: Simmer broth
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or fish stock and bring to a simmer. Add fish gently and season with salt and pepper.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Cook until the fish is done and the broth is glossy red. Keep it spoonable, not dry.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Ladle the chabeu over plain white rice or serve with cassava.
